focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Omar usually entered rooms as if he had already considered the possibility that he was imposing and decided, for everyone else's ease, not to perform the apology aloud. That morning the sixth-floor graduate office got a rougher version of him. The elevator opened. He stepped out with his scarf knotted wrong, one side hanging nearly to his knee, and a clear freezer bag under his arm because the navy accordion folder he used for official documents had split along the bottom seam. Inside the bag, a passport photocopy was bent around a stack of I-20s, and a packet of cardamom biscuits had burst somewhere along the walk over, leaving pale crumbs in the corners like grit.

Leela looked up from a paragraph she had been revising for forty minutes without improving. The radiator by the window had been knocking all morning in a patient, judicial way. Omar crossed to her cubicle too fast, then stopped too hard, as if his body had overshot the version of calm he meant to present.

"Do you have a minute," he said. Then, because even that was too imprecise for what was happening, "No. I need you to look at something now."

He set the bag on the desk. His hands were steady for one second and then not at all. Leela saw the tremor in the right one before he tucked it under his left wrist.

"What happened?"

"My funding letter isn't signed." He took out a stapled checklist printed from International Student Services. Every line had been highlighted in alternating yellow and blue. "The packet has to be complete by four-thirty or they treat the extension as late. They told me that three times in three slightly different sentences, which I assume means they enjoy it."

Leela read the checklist. It was the kind of administrative page universities produced by the thousands, all...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...had not told June, the older proof sitting in the dark behind this newer one like a second foundation.

"Mohit," she said, "we treat him like somebody who's already started building his explanation."

June considered that and nodded. "Okay."

For the first time all day, the answer did not feel like one more postponement. It felt like the beginning of method.

They stood there another moment in the alley cold, not touching. Traffic hissed on the wet street beyond the engineering buildings. Somewhere inside Keating a door alarm chirped and shut off. June pulled the folded note from her pocket, looked at it once more, and then tucked it into the inside compartment of her wallet behind an old MetroCard and three receipts.

"Tonight at seven," she said. "Text me only if you get delayed. Nothing else."

Leela said, "Okay."

June started walking toward the immigrant-commercial strip without waiting to see if Leela followed, confident now in a way that had nothing to do with optimism. It was simply direction. Leela fell in beside her after a step, matching pace.

Behind them, Keating kept humming, fluorescent and server-hot and falsely neutral. In her coat pocket Leela's phone pressed against her palm, warm from Mohit's text and the body heat she had given it. Ahead lay dinner, Omar, dates, copies, whatever name resistance took before it was strong enough to survive daylight. Beneath all of it, unchanged, sat the earlier secret she had never fully surrendered. She could feel its shape even as she moved.

But now there was a list. There were timestamps. There was a filed company with Mohit's name on it and a junior student in an expensive parka carrying messages he did not understand were messages. The paranoia had objects. That did not make the thing smaller. It made it usable.
